preFix.title: Chip butty
preFix.subtitle: Thick oven chips in soft white bread with butter and ketchup
preFix.excerpt: The chip butty is chips inside a bread roll or between two slices of white bread, with butter and ketchup. It is eaten across Britain without embarrassment, and the version made at home with good oven chips is better than almost any takeaway.
preFix.sourceNotes: A British working-class staple found across the North of England in particular but eaten nationally. Chip butties have been sold in fish and chip shops and made at home since the early 20th century. The combination of hot chips and buttered bread is a fixture of British comfort food.

content.text: The chip butty is chips in bread, and it needs no further justification. The chips should be thick, hot, and slightly soft in the middle. The bread should be soft and white. The butter melts into the chips. Ketchup goes on the inside of the bread, not after assembly. Eat before the chips cool.
Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Spread the frozen chips in a single layer on a baking tray. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes, turning once halfway through, until golden and just crisping at the edges.
Season the chips with sea salt.
Butter both slices of bread generously. Spread ketchup on one slice if using. Pile the hot chips onto the base, press the top piece of bread down so the butter meets the chips, and eat immediately.

items.fix: For a chip butty, pull the chips from the oven a minute or two before they are fully crisp. They should have some give.
items.cause: Oven chips baked until fully crispy rather than leaving them slightly soft.
items.symptom: The chips are too crisp and the bread tears when pressed

items.fix: Use a sensible quantity of chips and press the sandwich firmly together before eating.
items.cause: Too many chips or too much butter.
items.symptom: The sandwich is greasy and falls apart

items.fix: Spread chips in a single layer with space between them. Use two trays if needed.
items.symptom: The chips are pale and floppy

content.text: The chip butty is associated most strongly with the North of England but is eaten across the whole country without shame. It is one of the few British dishes that has never tried to become something else. The name itself is Northern dialect: a butty is any sandwich, from the same root as butter. There is no formal recipe and there never has been. Chips, bread, butter, and something from a bottle.
